summary: I was born in 1946 in Davis, California, which was a very small town back then. My dad worked at the School of Veterinary Medicine at UC Davis, and my mom was a kindergarten teacher. Growing up there gave me decent values about treating people well and being considerate of those less fortunate.

My dad was part of the School of Veterinary Medicine at the University of California, Davis, which influenced my early life. Growing up in Davis, with my dad working there, gave me a sense of community and decent values. UC Davis was part of my family background even before I went off to college.

When I was six years old, I lived in New Zealand for a year, which was one of the formative experiences of my childhood. It exposed me early on to the idea that the world was a large and complicated place. That year abroad helped shape my worldview from a young age.

In 1957, I lived for six months in Taiwan during a very tense period involving Taiwan and China. We even had an anti-aircraft gun outside our compound, which made me realize how complex and dangerous the world could be. That experience was another key moment in shaping how I saw global affairs.

During my time in Taiwan in 1957, the political situation with China was very heated. The presence of military defenses like the anti-aircraft gun near our compound highlighted the Cold War tensions in the region. This made me aware early on of the larger geopolitical struggles between China and Taiwan.

I went to Stanford University as a small-town kid and finished my degree there. While at Stanford, I also participated in Stanford in Italy, where I was exposed to progressive political ideas and groups like Students for a Democratic Society and the Black Panther Party. Those experiences, especially during the 1960s, helped shape my political awareness and opposition to the Vietnam War.

After graduating from Stanford, I joined the Peace Corps in 1968 and was sent to Somalia for an agriculture and rural development project. The project was poorly planned and a fiasco, so after six months, I shifted to sports and recreation in Mogadishu, teaching basketball to kids. The Peace Corps experience was eye-opening and coincided with major political upheavals in Somalia.

I spent time in Somalia in 1968 as a Peace Corps volunteer. Initially, I worked in a rural village under very basic conditions, but later moved to Mogadishu where I taught basketball. The country was undergoing political turmoil, including a military coup after the assassination of the president, and it was a Cold War hotspot with Soviet and U.S. involvement.

After six months in a rural village in Somalia, I moved to Mogadishu where I focused on sports and recreation, mainly teaching basketball to kids and organizing school teams. Mogadishu was a very different environment from home, with little contact with outsiders and a Muslim culture. It was also the center of political upheaval during my time there.

The year 1968 was pivotal for me; I chose to join the Peace Corps rather than the Army during the Vietnam War. That year I trained on the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation and then went to Somalia, where I experienced both the challenges of development work and the political turmoil of a coup. 1968 was a year that shaped my views on war, politics, and international development.

Our Peace Corps training in 1968 took place on the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation in North Dakota, along the Mississippi River. We built our own training facilities and infrastructure there, which was a tough but formative experience. It was an unusual place for training, but it prepared us for the challenges ahead in Somalia.

During Peace Corps training in 1968, we were on the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation, which is located on the banks of the Mississippi River. That setting was part of our early experience before heading to Somalia. It was a remote and challenging environment that helped prepare us for what was to come.

I remember arriving in a red International Harvester pickup truck when I first went to a village in Somalia during my Peace Corps service. It was a striking image because the locals didn’t understand English, and my greeting was met with confusion. That moment symbolized the communication and cultural challenges we faced.

Southern Somalia, where I was stationed, was formerly Italian Somaliland, which meant there was some colonial legacy but very little ongoing colonial presence during my time there. This history influenced the political and social context of the country. It was important to understand that Somalia was a newly independent nation with complex colonial roots.

During my time in Somalia, the Soviet Union was sponsoring the army, while the U.S. sponsored the national police, making Somalia a Cold War battleground. This geopolitical tension affected daily life and political events, including the military coup. It was a vivid example of how global superpowers influenced local conflicts.

I was born and raised in the United States, specifically in Davis, California. Later, I trained for the Peace Corps in the U.S. on the Standing Rock Sioux Reservation and returned to work in various legal services roles in American cities like Seattle, Everett, and Mount Vernon. The U.S. was both my home and the backdrop for much of my education and professional life.

While at Stanford, I spent time at Stanford University in Italy, living in a fraternity house and meeting a diverse group of students. It was there that I was exposed to progressive political ideas and groups like Students for a Democratic Society and the Black Panther Party. That experience broadened my political perspective during the 1960s.

At Stanford in Italy, I encountered members of Students for a Democratic Society, a progressive political group active in the 1960s. Their ideas challenged me to think differently about politics and social justice. Although I didn’t immediately act on it, their presence planted seeds of political awareness that grew over time.

During my time at Stanford in Italy, I was introduced to the Black Panther Party through conversations with fellow students. I was told they were not what the press portrayed, which made me reconsider my assumptions. This exposure contributed to my evolving political consciousness in the late 1960s.

I attended Yale Law School starting in the fall of 1970, where I was classmates with Bill Clinton and knew Hillary Clinton, who was a year ahead. Yale was a very liberal environment that encouraged me to think politically and socially. It was there that I became truly politicized, working in Legal Services and engaging with issues of poverty and justice.

Bill Clinton was a classmate of mine at Yale Law School when I started in 1970. We were part of a cohort that included future senators, judges, and deans. Although I was politically active and liberal, I knew I didn’t quite fit in with the mainstream worldview even then.

Hillary Clinton was a year ahead of me at Yale Law School when I started in 1970. She was part of the same liberal and politically engaged environment that shaped much of my thinking. Being at Yale during that time put me in contact with many future influential people.

While at Yale Law School in New Haven, I worked part-time in Legal Services in the city’s ghetto. That experience exposed me to the harsh realities of poverty, including people living in homes with holes in the floors. It was a profound and eye-opening time that shaped my commitment to social justice.

After my first year of law school, I interned at the Community Law Firm in San Francisco, which was openly Marxist. At first, I didn’t understand why they had pictures of Chinese peasants on the wall, but over time I grasped their internationalist revolutionary perspective. That experience helped me start thinking like a revolutionary.

During law school, I helped start a chapter of the National Lawyers Guild, a left-wing national lawyers organization with student chapters. This involvement deepened my political engagement and commitment to social justice causes. The Guild was an important part of my legal and political development.

I audited a Spanish conversation class during law school to improve my speaking skills, since I had learned some Spanish growing up in California but wasn’t very fluent. The class was informal and political, which added another layer to my education. It helped me communicate better in later work with Latino communities.

I met my wife, Sasha Harmon, at Yale Law School; she was a year ahead of me. After graduating, she moved to Seattle to work for Legal Services, and I followed a year later. We worked together in legal services and community law, and she was an important partner throughout my career.

After law school, I moved to Seattle where my wife Sasha was working in Legal Services. I also worked in Legal Services in nearby Everett and Mount Vernon before returning to Seattle. Seattle became home to much of my legal and community work in the 1970s.

I worked for Legal Services in Everett, Washington, for about six to eight months after moving to the Pacific Northwest. This was part of my early legal career serving low-income communities. Everett was one of the cities where I gained practical experience in community law.

Along with Everett, I worked for Legal Services in Mount Vernon, Washington, for several months. These experiences helped me understand the legal needs of rural and working-class populations. Mount Vernon was part of my early career in public interest law.

In the mid-1970s, I worked on the southern tip of Texas near Brownsville, serving a predominantly Latino community. This was a challenging and eye-opening experience where I witnessed institutional and overt racism firsthand. It was one of the most interesting and important jobs of my career.

I spent about two and a half years working near Brownsville, Texas, in a community that was 80 percent Latino. The work involved representing Mexican-Americans with various legal problems, blending farm worker issues with civil rights. It was a formative period that exposed me to the realities of racial and economic injustice.

While working in Texas, I encountered the Farm Workers Union, which was experiencing internal splits. Although I was involved in legal work related to farm workers and civil rights, I distanced myself from the political infighting within the union. The union’s struggles highlighted the complexities of labor organizing in that era.

My wife Sasha had a standing offer to work as a lawyer for the Skokomish Indian Tribe when we returned to Seattle. This connection brought us closer to Native American legal and community issues. The Skokomish Tribe represented another important community in our legal and social justice work.
